message:
feat(core/insert-select): streaming fallback path + extracted row-stream replay

Introduce a streaming fallback for `INSERT ... SELECT` so large
inserts do not have to materialize the full source row set before
the first write lands. Refactors the previously-monolithic
`execute_insert_select_materialized_rows` into a row-stream
primitive that both the materialized and streaming paths share.

## New helpers

- `try_execute_streaming_insert_select_fallback(insert, select,
  params)` attempts the streaming path first. Returns
  `Ok(Some(changes))` if streaming was applicable (simple SELECT
  shape, no RETURNING, no conflict with the insert target), or
  `Ok(None)` to fall through to the existing materialized path.
  `execute_insert_select_materialized` now tries this first so the
  materialized path only runs when streaming cannot.

- `execute_insert_select_row_stream(insert, producer)` is the
  shared replay primitive. It takes a row producer closure
  (`FnMut(&mut dyn FnMut(&[SqliteValue]) -> Result<()>) -> Result<()>`)
  and runs the prepared insert replay once per emitted row.
  `execute_insert_select_materialized_rows` now becomes a thin
  wrapper that pushes its pre-materialized rows through this
  stream primitive, and the new streaming fallback feeds rows
  into the same primitive directly from the source SELECT.

- `build_insert_select_replay_sql(insert, layout)` extracts the
  SQL construction (qualified table, placeholders, conflict
  clause, RETURNING validation) out of the old monolithic
  `execute_insert_select_materialized_rows` so both the
  materialized and streaming paths build identical replay SQL
  from the same code.

- `streaming_insert_select_orders_by_primary_rowid(...)` is the
  shape check that decides whether a SELECT is stream-replayable:
  it must preserve primary rowid ordering so the streaming path
  can safely interleave reads and writes against the target table
  without tripping the "don't mutate the table you're iterating"
  rule.

- `build_streaming_hash_join_index(...)` and
  `stream_hash_join_rows(producer)` are free functions that
  construct an in-memory hash-join index from one side of the
  join and stream joined rows from the other side. Used by the
  streaming fallback when the source SELECT contains a simple
  equi-join whose build side fits in memory. The probe side is
  streamed row-by-row so only one side of the join has to be
  materialized.

## Behavior

- Small inserts (no join, single table source) still use the
  existing materialized path — the streaming fallback declines
  via `Ok(None)` so there is no regression on the common case.
- Large `INSERT ... SELECT ... FROM t` with no RETURNING now
  streams: each source row is replayed through the prepared
  insert before the next row is fetched, so peak memory does
  not scale with the source row count.
- Large `INSERT ... SELECT ... FROM a JOIN b ON ...` streams
  with a single side materialized as a hash index, matching
  the SQLite behavior for equivalent plans.
- `INSERT ... SELECT ... RETURNING` continues to use the
  materialized path because RETURNING needs the full row set
  to produce its result, and the streaming primitive rejects
  it up front with a `debug_assert!` as documentation.
